ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for packaged fruit smoothie; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve packaged fruit smoothie bottle or carton of approximately 250 ml, equivalent to 250 g, representing one standard retail serving.

nutrient_source.title: Fruit smoothie (packaged, any flavour) — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for packaged fruit smoothie (any flavour), CR ID D03060003, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — fruit juice and blended fruit beverage analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for a generic packaged fruit smoothie in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 59 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: blended mixed-fruit juice / fruit drink (GI ~59, medium category) per Foster-Powell et al. 2002 (Am J Clin Nutr 76:5–56). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (59) × available carbohydrate per serving (31.25 g per 250 g serving) / 100 = 18.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (11.23 g/100 g) in a liquid beverage format exceeds the 5 g/100 g sugar gate for sugar-sweetened beverages (SSBs), independently elevating blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines regardless of the low sodium content (26 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this packaged fruit smoothie due to its SSB sugar level (11.23 g/100 g); choose freshly blended whole-fruit smoothies with no added sugar or plain water instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Liquid sugar at 11.23 g/100 g with negligible dietary fibre (0.3 g/100 g) results in rapid glucose absorption and significant postprandial glycaemic excursion; medium GI (59) combined with high glycaemic load per serving (18.4) makes this beverage unsuitable for blood glucose management per ADA 2023 guidelines.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this packaged fruit smoothie; the high sugar load and low fibre content cause rapid blood glucose spikes — opt for whole fruit or freshly blended unsweetened smoothies with visible pulp and fibre.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free-sugar content (11.23 g/100 g) in a liquid beverage contributes to elevated triglycerides, visceral adiposity, and cardiometabolic risk when consumed regularly; fat content is low (0.32 g/100 g) and sodium is low (26 mg/100 g), partially mitigating direct lipid and blood pressure risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular health, limit intake of this packaged fruit smoothie due to its high free-sugar content which raises triglyceride and cardiometabolic risk; the American Heart Association recommends limiting added sugar to less than 25 g/day for women and 36 g/day for men.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar at 11.23 g/100 g with negligible fibre (0.3 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity with no qualifying anti-inflammatory factor present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this packaged fruit smoothie as its high sugar content promotes systemic inflammation; choose unsweetened whole-fruit alternatives rich in polyphenols and fibre.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged fruit smoothie (any flavour) is a ready-to-drink blended fruit beverage sold in sealed bottles or cartons, typically containing fruit puree, fruit juice concentrate, water, and added sugars or natural fruit sugars. It has a thick, creamy texture and a sweet, fruity flavour profile that varies by flavour variant (e.g., mango, strawberry, mixed berry, tropical). Per 100 g, it provides 61 kcal, 1.63 g protein, 0.32 g fat, 12.5 g carbohydrate, 11.23 g sugar, 0.3 g dietary fibre, 26 mg sodium, and 71 mg calcium. The high sugar content (11.23 g/100 g) and low fibre (0.3 g/100 g) make this a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) by clinical classification. Unlike freshly blended whole-fruit smoothies that retain intact fibre and phytonutrients, packaged versions are ultra-processed and typically contain added sugars or fruit concentrates that cause rapid glycaemic spikes. This product is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its SSB sugar profile. It is plant-based, dairy-free, and gluten-free. Commonly consumed as a snack or on-the-go beverage in Singapore and Malaysia. Clinically, the liquid sugar format accelerates glucose absorption compared to whole fruit, making portion control and label scrutiny essential.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in a packaged fruit smoothie?
Is fruit smoothie good for high blood pressure?
Is bottled fruit smoothie the same as fresh smoothie?
Is packaged smoothie a sugar-sweetened beverage?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (CR ID D03060003) with a reported confidence of 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved source (University of Sydney GI Database / Foster-Powell et al. 2002) and clearly documented as an analogous estimate.
notes: iron_mg is 0.0 per the HPB source (confirmed zero — HPB reports 0.0 mg iron per 100 g for this product). saturated_fat_g is 0.0 per HPB source (confirmed zero — HPB reports 0.0 g saturated fat per 100 g). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this generic packaged fruit smoothie category are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D03060003. GI of 59 is an analogous estimate based on blended mixed-fruit juice / fruit drink from Foster-Powell et al. 2002 (Am J Clin Nutr 76:5–56) via the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI entry exists for a generic packaged fruit smoothie. This is a generic record covering any flavour variant; actual nutrient values may vary by specific product and flavour. Consumers should check individual product labels for precise nutritional information.
